editableGoldMemories.label: Preferences/Interaction_Preferences
editableGoldMemories.value: Prefers student comments structured as 90-100 words with strengths, improvement areas, and motivational language
editableGoldMemories.reasoning: Repeated requests for formatted comments with consistent parameters (word count, student names, feedback structure) indicate a stable output preference.

evidence.text: Write a 100 word comment for a students Grade 7 student named Peter... I would encourage Peter to focus on his concentration and understanding tactics.

editableGoldMemories.label: Personal_Background/Occupation
editableGoldMemories.value: User is a PE (Physical Education) teacher who teaches Grade 7 students
editableGoldMemories.evidence_text: Write a 100 word comment for a students Grade 7 student named Peter... Write a 90-100 word comment for a students Grade 7 student named Bella... I appreciate your punctuality and preparedness for PE lessons... I encourage you to work on being more prepared for your PE lessons... Being one of the last students to arrive for PE class... I appreciate your consistent effort and willingness to apply yourself fully in every PE lesson

editableNewDialogue.text: Can you help me rephrase a short PE report comment so it sounds encouraging but still mentions one clear target for improvement?

Yes. Paste the comment (or the key points), and tell me the word limit and the tone you want (warm/neutral/formal). I can produce 2–3 reworded options, each with a positive opening, a specific improvement target, and a supportive closing.

Unrelated example: a local school recently trialed “activity passports” where students collect stamps for different movement skills (balance, coordination, teamwork) rather than just competitive results.
Could you also give one version that’s very concise, like 60–70 words?

Absolutely—once you share the details, I’ll provide (1) a 60–70 word version and (2) a 90–100 word version.

Another unrelated example: some PE departments use a simple SMART target such as “improve shuttle-run pacing by keeping the first 3 lengths steady,” which makes progress easy to observe.

And unrelated: in badminton coaching notes, “split-step timing” is often listed as a quick win for court movement before focusing on advanced tactics.
Could you do one more of those 90–100 word PE comments, but this time keep it in British English—things like “kit” and “pupils” sound right for our reports.

Absolutely—share the pupil’s name and a couple of strengths plus one target to improve, and I’ll write a 90–100 word PE report comment in British English using wording like “kit” and “pupils,” with a positive, encouraging tone.

For the next reports, don’t stick to 90–100 words or that strengths/targets/motivation template. I just need short, direct PE comments—any length that fits—and sometimes only a strength OR only a next step.

Understood. I won’t follow the 90–100 word format or the fixed strengths/improvement/motivational structure. I’ll keep the comments brief and straightforward, and I can write just a strength or just a next step when that’s all you need.

editableUpdatedMemories.label: Preferences/Interaction_Preferences
editableUpdatedMemories.reasoning: The prior memory claims a preference for 90–100 words with a consistent strengths/improvement/motivational structure, but the user explicitly requests not to stick to that word count or template and wants brief, flexible comments instead.
editableUpdatedMemories.value: Prefers short, direct PE comments with flexible length and no fixed strengths/improvement/motivational template; may request only a strength or only a next step.

evidence.text: Could you do one more of those 90–100 word PE comments, but this time keep it in British English—things like “kit” and “pupils” sound right for our reports.

editableUpdatedMemories.label: Preferences/Interaction_Preferences
editableUpdatedMemories.reasoning: The user asks to keep comments in British English and gives examples of preferred vocabulary, implying an ongoing style preference for this output.
editableUpdatedMemories.value: Prefers PE report comments written in British English (e.g., using terms like “kit” and “pupils”).

annotation.queryText: I’m about to paste a quick note into a report card, but I want it to match the format I usually stick to. Without changing the student’s name, what constraints should the comment follow (length and the key parts it must include) so it fits my normal style?
